message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 20
Recent Green phase: No implementation change needed — the loyalty boundary test (exactly 2 years → loyalty applies, staff → 77 G) was already green; the threshold uses >= 2.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. If no refactoring is warranted, document why.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 20 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation against the Four Rules of Simple Design.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent Green phase added no implementation change — the loyalty boundary test was already green via `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD`. So I evaluate the existing names.

Reviewing each name against current behavior:

- `runScenario`, `quotePremium`, `policyBasePremium`, `typeBasePremium`, `formsBlock`, `countByType` — all clearly reveal intent.
- `itemSurcharge`, `itemSurchargeRate`, `itemSurcharges` — clear.
- `policyModifierRate`, `applyModifierRate`, `roundUpPremium` — clear.
- `sumApplicableRates`, `RateRule` — clear and well-documented.
- `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD = 2`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`, etc. — clear.

The naming is genuinely strong. No rename improves intent.

## Step 2: Evaluate Rules 1–4

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 20 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Already high. The code reads cleanly with explanatory comments on the non-obvious bits (modifier scope, rounding rationale, follow-up derivation).

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** Let me look carefully. `COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM = 25` is referenced in `BASE_PREMIUMS` for `rune` and `moonstone`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` separately lists those same two types. There is a conceptual coupling: the set of component types and the entries that use `COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM` represent the same knowledge ("which item types are components"). However, deriving one from the other (e.g. `COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(Object.keys(BASE_PREMIUMS).filter(t => BASE_PREMIUMS[t] === COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM))`) would couple "is a component" to "happens to cost 25 G," which is incidental, not definitional — that would *hurt* Rule 2 (intent). So I'll leave that as-is.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Looking at `quotePremium` — there's a one-time-use local `policyBase` that is immediately consumed. It aids readability, so keep it.

Let me look for a genuine, safe improvement. In `quotePremium`, the intermediate `const { items } = step;` destructure is fine. But I notice `modifiedPolicyBase` line exceeds the visual rhythm of the rest. More substantively: the comment on line 134's logic is split — let me find the single highest-value, low-risk refactor.

The strongest candidate is a small **Rule 2 / Rule 4** improvement: `itemSurchargeRate` is a one-line indirection (`sumApplicableRates(SURCHARGE_RULES, item)`) used only by `itemSurcharge`. Inlining it removes an element without losing clarity, since `itemSurcharge` already names the concept.

Let me calculate APP mass before/after for this change.

**Current mass (the two functions):**
```
const itemSurchargeRate = (item: Item): number =>
  sumApplicableRates(SURCHARGE_RULES, item);
const itemSurcharge = (item: Item): number =>
  itemSurchargeRate(item) * BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type];
```
- `itemSurchargeRate`: binding `item` (1) + invocation `sumApplicableRates` (2) + binding `SURCHARGE_RULES` (1) + binding `item` (1) = 5
- `itemSurcharge`: binding `item` (1) + invocation `itemSurchargeRate` (2) + binding `BASE_PREMIUMS` (1) + invocation `[item.type]` index ~ binding (1) + binding `item` (1) = 6
- Total ≈ 11

Wait — on reflection, `itemSurchargeRate` mirrors the structure of `policyModifierRate` (both are `sumApplicableRates(RULES, subject)` wrappers). That symmetry is itself intent-revealing: "rate" functions wrap the rule engine, and "surcharge/amount" functions multiply by a base. Removing only one half of the pair breaks the symmetry and *reduces* clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 4). I'll **not** inline it.

Let me reconsider the multi-item modifier scoping for a real simplification. Reading `quotePremium`:

The mandate is to attempt at least one refactoring. I've found that most candidate changes would *harm* clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP). But there is one genuinely positive change in `quotePremium`: line 134 is the longest line in the file and reads densely because it nests `policyModifierRate(context)` inside `applyModifierRate(...)`. Extracting an explaining variable `modifierRate` makes the two-step "compute the rate, then apply it" intent explicit and matches the prose in the comment ("Policy modifiers scale the policy base").

## Step 3 / Step 4: Apply the refactoring (Rule 2 — reveals intent)

**Before mass (lines 131, 134):**
- `policyBase` binding(1) = invocation `policyBasePremium`(2) + binding `items`(1) → 4
- `modifiedPolicyBase` binding(1) = invocation `applyModifierRate`(2) + binding `policyBase`(1) + invocation `policyModifierRate`(2) + binding `context`(1) → 7
- Subtotal = 11

**After** (split the nested call into an explaining variable):

content.text:
**After mass:**
- `policyBase` binding(1) = invocation(2) + binding(1) → 4
- `modifierRate` binding(1) = invocation `policyModifierRate`(2) + binding `context`(1) → 4
- `modifiedPolicyBase` binding(1) = invocation `applyModifierRate`(2) + binding `policyBase`(1) + binding `modifierRate`(1) → 5
- Subtotal = 13

Mass rose slightly (11 → 13) because the explaining variable adds one binding. Per the guidance, **Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP**: the change names the intermediate "rate" concept, flattens the longest/densest line in the file, and makes the comment's "Policy modifiers scale the policy base" map directly onto `applyModifierRate(policyBase, modifierRate)`.

content.text:
All 20 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied:** Extracted an explaining variable `modifierRate` in `quotePremium` (`src/claim-office.ts`, line ~132), splitting the previously nested `applyModifierRate(policyBase, policyModifierRate(context))` into a named two-step "compute the rate, then apply it" sequence.

**Rule applied:** Rule 2 (Reveals Intent). This was the highest-value change available — it flattens the file's longest, densest line and makes the adjacent comment ("Policy modifiers scale the policy base") map directly onto the now-readable `applyModifierRate(policyBase, modifierRate)`.

**APP mass:** 11 → 13 (Δ +2) for the affected lines, from the added binding. Per the APP guidance, **Rule 2 trumps low mass**: the small mass increase buys clearer intent.

**Candidates rejected (and why):**
- **Naming:** All names already reveal intent — no rename improves clarity.
- **Inline `itemSurchargeRate`** (would lower mass / fewer elements): rejected because it mirrors `policyModifierRate` as a `sumApplicableRates` wrapper; that symmetry is intent-revealing, so removing one half would hurt Rule 2.
- **Derive `COMPONENT_TYPES` from `BASE_PREMIUMS`** (would remove conceptual duplication, Rule 3): rejected because it would couple "is a component" to the incidental fact that components happen to cost 25 G, weakening intent.

**Tests:** All 20 passing (25 todo remain).
